evals.name: drift-resolution-edited-target
evals.prompt: I edited ~/.zshrc directly to add `alias gst='git status'`. The chezmoi source is at home/dot_zshrc (a plain file, not a template). Get this back into source without losing anything.
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes the edited-target situation, runs `chezmoi source-path` as a required pre-check, then `chezmoi re-add` (NOT `chezmoi add --force`, NOT manual copy), reads the diff via the `- dest / + target` direction convention, and closes with a `chezmoi cd` + `git diff --check` handoff before commit.
expectations: Recommends `chezmoi re-add ~/.zshrc` rather than `chezmoi add --force` or copying the file by hand; warns that `chezmoi apply` would delete the live edit

Performs `chezmoi source-path ~/.zshrc` as a REQUIRED pre-check before re-add (not optional, not an afterthought), to confirm the source is a regular file and not a special prefix type that re-add would silently skip
Uses the `- dest / + target` direction convention from workflow.md when describing `chezmoi diff` output (or uses the `diff <(chezmoi cat ~/.zshrc) ~/.zshrc` sidestep recipe to bypass +/- confusion)
Includes `git diff --check` as a pre-commit/handoff step (whitespace sanity), and uses `chezmoi cd` to enter the source directory for the git-side commit
Does not suggest `chezmoi destroy` or any destructive command; does not suggest `chezmoi add` on a `.tmpl` source
Cites the workflow.md drift-resolution decision tree (or the matching Universal Rule from SKILL.md) by name, not just generic chezmoi knowledge

evals.name: plist-template-literal-escaping
evals.prompt: This plist template at home/.chezmoitemplates/com.example.geo.plist.tmpl has `<string>{{width}}x{{height}}+0+0</string>` which is meant as a literal Moom-style geometry string, not Go template variables. Fix it so chezmoi apply does not try to resolve `width` and `height`.
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes the literal-vs-template ambiguity, escapes the literal `{{` and `}}` (preferably with the backtick literal form for whole-string clarity), and confirms the fix with `chezmoi execute-template`.
expectations: Identifies that `{{width}}` would be interpreted as a Go template variable lookup and fail
Applies an escape: backtick literal `{{ \`{{width}}x{{height}}+0+0\` }}` OR the explicit `{{ "{{" }}` form
Escapes ALL six literal placeholders in the fixture: `{{width}}`, `{{height}}`, `{{topleft}}`, `{{topright}}`, `{{bottomleft}}`, `{{bottomright}}` — not just the geometry string
Leaves the legitimate `{{ .chezmoi.username }}` and `{{ .chezmoi.hostname }}` directives unchanged
References the Moom-style precedent or the repo rule that plist literals must be escaped
Suggests `chezmoi execute-template < home/.chezmoitemplates/com.example.geo.plist.tmpl` to confirm the fix renders cleanly (no `function ... not defined` errors)
Suggests `make test-plist-hooks` as the validation lane
Does not move the file to home/.chezmoiassets/ (templating is still desired for other parts of the file)

evals.name: secrets-toml-obfuscated-op-refs
evals.prompt: Add an op:// reference for my Anthropic API key (1Password item name 'Anthropic', field 'credential') to home/.chezmoidata/secrets.toml. The vault is 'Personal'.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses the obfuscated form (vault-id/item-id/field-id), explicitly NOT the human-readable `op://Personal/Anthropic/credential`, and explains the rule. It points the user at `~/.config/chezmoi/chezmoi.toml.local` as the place for human-readable refs.
expectations: Adds the new key under the `[secrets.refs]` table (NOT at top level — templates read `.secrets.refs.<name>`)
Refuses to write a human-readable `op://Personal/Anthropic/credential` form into the committed file
Asks for or shows how to obtain the obfuscated vault-id, item-id, and field-id (e.g., via `op item get Anthropic --vault Personal --format=json`)
Notes that human-readable refs go in `~/.config/chezmoi/chezmoi.toml.local`, which is not committed
References the repo rule about obfuscated `op://` refs only in committed source
Suggests `make test-secret-backed-files` as the validation lane
Notes that `secrets_enabled` (in `chezmoi.toml`) controls whether the template renders, not the env var on every apply

evals.name: meta-skill-update-new-env-var
evals.prompt: We just added a new env var DOTFILES_INSTALL_NIX=true that opts into Nix package management when chezmoi apply runs. Update the chezmoi-management skill at chezmoi-management/ to reflect this.
evals.expected_output: The agent reads `references/meta-skill-maintenance.md` first, identifies `references/packages-and-secrets.md` as the env var table's home, updates the table, decides whether the rule is high-stakes enough to also list in `SKILL.md` Repo-Specific Gotchas, and runs the post-edit validation checklist.
expectations: Loads or references `references/meta-skill-maintenance.md` before editing
Updates the env var table in `references/packages-and-secrets.md` with `DOTFILES_INSTALL_NIX`
Considers whether to also add the env var to `SKILL.md` Repo-Specific Gotchas; explains the threshold (high-stakes / destructive) for inclusion
Does NOT touch SKILL.md Mode Router (no new mode introduced)
Runs the post-edit validation checklist (frontmatter parse, openai.yaml parse, evals.json parse, mode router cross-check)
Does not paraphrase chezmoi.io docs into the skill (self-contained rule)

evals.name: re-add-skips-tmpl-source
evals.prompt: I edited ~/.zshrc directly to add `alias k=kubectl`. The chezmoi source is `home/dot_zshrc.tmpl` (it's templated to swap in a different prompt theme per hostname). Get my edit back into source. I'd normally just `chezmoi re-add ~/.zshrc` — does that work here?
evals.expected_output: The agent runs `chezmoi source-path ~/.zshrc` first, identifies the source as `.tmpl`, and explains that `chezmoi re-add` silently SKIPS templates (does not capture the live edit). It refuses both `re-add` and `add --force` for this case and recommends manually porting the alias into the `.tmpl` outside any conditional block, then `chezmoi diff ~/.zshrc` to confirm.
expectations: Runs or describes `chezmoi source-path ~/.zshrc` as the pre-check
Identifies the source as a `.tmpl` and explains `chezmoi re-add` SKIPS templates — it does NOT capture the live edit into the .tmpl

Enumerates non-regular-file re-add disqualifiers beyond `.tmpl` (at least 3 of: `symlink_`, `modify_`, `create_`, `run_*`, `remove_`). Mentioning only `.tmpl` is insufficient. NOTE: `encrypted_` regular files ARE safe for re-add (it re-encrypts via the configured key), so encrypted_ should NOT appear on the disqualifier list.
Explicitly refuses `chezmoi re-add ~/.zshrc` for this case and `chezmoi add --force` (would clobber the template with rendered output)
Cites SKILL.md Universal Rule 3 (destructive command discipline) or the workflow.md drift-resolution decision tree `.tmpl` branch by name, not generic chezmoi knowledge
Recommends manual port outside the host-conditional block, then `chezmoi diff <target>` to confirm
Suggests `chezmoi edit ~/.zshrc` as the editor entry point (handles .tmpl extension automatically)
Agent's instructions actually result in source being updated (does not assume re-add captured it)

evals.name: assets-vs-templates-literal-json
evals.prompt: VoiceInk's prompts live in home/.chezmoitemplates/voiceink-prompts.json with literal `{{user_input}}` / `{{ context }}` placeholders that VoiceInk fills at runtime. The plist fragment at home/.chezmoitemplates/com.prakashjoshipax.VoiceInk.plist.tmpl currently base64-includes that JSON via `{{ includeTemplate "voiceink-prompts.json" | b64enc }}` — chezmoi apply fails because Go template tries to resolve `user_input`/`context`. Fix it. Don't touch the modify_ stub at home/Library/private_Preferences/modify_private_com.prakashjoshipax.VoiceInk.plist.tmpl — that one's a standard 3-line stub.
evals.expected_output: The agent fixes the PLIST FRAGMENT (not the modify_ stub) by switching `includeTemplate` to `include` for the JSON payload. Either (a) move JSON to `home/.chezmoiassets/` and use `{{ include ".chezmoiassets/voiceink-prompts.json" | b64enc }}`, or (b) keep it under `home/.chezmoitemplates/` and use `{{ include (printf "%s/.chezmoitemplates/voiceink-prompts.json" .chezmoi.sourceDir) | b64enc }}` (the real-repo VoiceInk pattern). Notes that `include` reads bytes verbatim and that the literal `{{user_input}}` etc. survive into the b64-encoded data value.
expectations: Identifies that the bug is in the PLIST FRAGMENT (`home/.chezmoitemplates/com.prakashjoshipax.VoiceInk.plist.tmpl`), NOT in the modify_ stub. Does not edit the modify_ stub.
Identifies that `includeTemplate` would try to evaluate every `{{user_input}}` and `{{context}}` in the JSON and fail

Recommends one of two valid approaches for the FRAGMENT: move JSON to `home/.chezmoiassets/` + `include`, OR keep in `home/.chezmoitemplates/` + `include` with the `printf .chezmoi.sourceDir` form. Both forms keep the `| b64enc` so the JSON lands as the `<data>` value.
Notes that `include` resolves paths relative to the chezmoi source directory, NOT relative to `.chezmoitemplates/`
Does NOT propose escaping the literal `{{user_input}}` / `{{context}}` braces (wrong tool for a raw payload)
References the real-repo VoiceInk precedent in `references/app-config.md`
Suggests `chezmoi execute-template < <fragment>` (or against the stub) to verify
Distinguishes asset (raw bytes, `include`) from template (Go template, `includeTemplate`)

evals.name: mas-app-opt-in-gate
evals.prompt: Add Things 3 (App Store ID 904280696) to my package list at home/.chezmoidata/packages.toml so I get it on every fresh machine.
evals.expected_output:
The agent adds the entry under a profile's `mas = [ ... ]` array using inline-table syntax with id as an integer, NOT under `casks`. It classifies MAS as gated on `DOTFILES_INSTALL_MAS_APPS=true` (apply-time, not init-prompt) — installs are not unconditional. It asks whether the user wants this on `core`, `full`, or both, and suggests verifying with `scripts/packages/render-brewfile --profile

--include-mas` (the wrapper strips a bare env var, so `--include-mas` is the only way to see MAS lines in the rendered output).
expectations: Adds the entry under a profile's `mas = [...]` inline array using `{ name = "Things 3", id = 904280696 }` (integer id, no quotes). Does NOT add under casks/brews, does NOT use array-of-tables `[[packages.profiles.full.mas]]`, does NOT add at top level of `[packages]`

Classifies `DOTFILES_INSTALL_MAS_APPS` as an APPLY-time gate (read on every `chezmoi apply` via env template function), NOT an init-time prompt persisted to `chezmoi.toml`. The user must export the var on each invocation, not just at init.
Asks which profile(s) (`core`, `full`, or both) and flags the both-profiles pitfall (profiles don't inherit)

Suggests a GATED render verification using `scripts/packages/render-brewfile --profile

--include-mas` (the wrapper flag — the bare `DOTFILES_INSTALL_MAS_APPS=true` env-form does NOT work because the wrapper script clears that var with `env -u`). Plain `make test-render-brewfile` or `render-brewfile --profile

` without the flag is insufficient because they omit `mas` lines entirely.
Specifically names the repo's `default_profile = "full"` setting (the TOML key, not just the concept) when selecting which profile to update
References the apply-time vs init-prompt split documented in `references/packages-and-secrets.md` (e.g., explicitly mentions the apply-time env var table or the two-flavors-of-env-var distinction)

evals.name: chezmoidata-cannot-template
evals.prompt: Make home/.chezmoidata/packages.toml conditional on hostname so my work laptop gets a different brew list than my personal one. I figured I'd just rename it to packages.toml.tmpl and add `{{ if eq .chezmoi.hostname "work" }}` blocks.
evals.expected_output: The agent refuses the rename and explains: `home/.chezmoidata/` files load BEFORE the template engine starts; they cannot themselves be templates. It offers correct alternatives: per-profile separation via `DOTFILES_INSTALL_PROFILE` (the existing repo pattern), or per-host data via `home/.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` [data] section, or `home/.chezmoiignore` to gate files by host. It does NOT propose any solution where a `.chezmoidata/*.tmpl` file exists.
expectations: Refuses the rename to `.tmpl` and explains: `home/.chezmoidata/` loads BEFORE the template engine
Cites the rule from SKILL.md Universal Rules (Rule 4) or packages-and-secrets.md
Offers at least one correct alternative: per-profile via `DOTFILES_INSTALL_PROFILE`, OR per-host data injected via `home/.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` [data], OR file-level gating via `home/.chezmoiignore`
Recommends per-profile separation (`DOTFILES_INSTALL_PROFILE`) when the use case is package-set selection per machine, since that matches the existing repo pattern
Does NOT propose any solution where a file under `home/.chezmoidata/` has a `.tmpl` extension
Mentions `home/.chezmoi.<format>.tmpl` (at the source root, not under .chezmoidata/) as the place for dynamic chezmoi-data values if needed

evals.name: secrets-toml-top-level-rejected
evals.prompt:
Quick PR review: someone added this to home/.chezmoidata/secrets.toml:

```toml
github_token = "op://abcd1234efgh5678/wxyz9999aaaa/credential"
```

Looks fine to me — does it work?
evals.expected_output: The agent identifies that the entry is at the top level of the file, not under `[secrets.refs]`. Templates read `.secrets.refs.<name>` via `onepasswordRead`, so a top-level key won't be reachable. It recommends moving the entry under `[secrets.refs]` and suggests `chezmoi data --format=yaml | grep -i secrets` to verify the loaded shape. It does NOT suggest changing the template-side code path to read top-level.
expectations: Identifies that the new entry is at the top level of the file, not under `[secrets.refs]`
Explains that templates resolve via `.secrets.refs.<name>` (e.g. `onepasswordRead .secrets.refs.github_token`) — top-level keys won't be reachable
Recommends moving the entry under the existing `[secrets.refs]` table
Confirms the obfuscated `op://vault-id/item-id/field-id` form itself is correct (does not conflate with the human-readable-form rule)
Suggests `chezmoi data --format=yaml | grep -i secrets` (or equivalent) to verify the loaded shape
Suggests `make test-secret-backed-files` as the validation lane
Does NOT propose changing template-side code to read top-level (wrong direction of fix)

evals.name: source-target-translation-prefix-order
evals.prompt: I want my SSH config to be mode 0600 and live at ~/.ssh/config. I created home/dot_private_ssh/dot_private_config but chezmoi apply puts the file at the WRONG path — it ended up at ~/.private_ssh/.private_config with default 0644 instead of ~/.ssh/config with 0600. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes that `dot_` precedes `private_` in the path, so chezmoi strips `dot_` first and treats the remaining `private_ssh` / `private_config` as literal name segments — resulting in `~/.private_ssh/.private_config` with default mode. The agent recommends renaming to `home/private_dot_ssh/private_config` (private_ before dot_ in the directory; NO dot_ on the file since target is `config`, not `.config`) and verifies with `chezmoi target-path`. Does NOT suggest a chmod workaround.
expectations: Identifies that `dot_private_ssh` / `dot_private_config` puts `dot_` BEFORE `private_`, violating the strict prefix order. Correctly explains chezmoi strips `dot_` first, leaving `private_ssh` and `private_config` as literal name segments — so the target is `~/.private_ssh/.private_config`, NOT `~/.ssh/config` with wrong mode.

Recommends renaming the directory to `private_dot_ssh/` (private_ before dot_) and the FILE to `private_config` (no `dot_` on the file — user wants `~/.ssh/config`, not `~/.ssh/.config`). `private_dot_config` would incorrectly target `.config` under `~/.ssh/`.
Cites or paraphrases the strict per-target-type prefix order from `references/source-target-translation.md`
Suggests `chezmoi target-path home/private_dot_ssh/private_config` to confirm it resolves to `~/.ssh/config`
Suggests `chezmoi managed` to confirm chezmoi sees the renamed entry
Does NOT propose a post-apply `chmod` workaround or any other hack that bypasses the prefix system
Mentions that wrong order = chezmoi silently ignores the prefix (silent-fail), not an explicit error

evals.name: script-numeric-ordering-insert-at-gap
evals.prompt: I'm adding a run_onchange_after_ script that installs a Rust toolchain via rustup. It needs to run after Homebrew (10-brew-bundle) and before mise-install (20-mise-install) so mise can manage the rust version. What should I name it?
evals.expected_output: The agent picks a number in the gap between 10 and 20 that doesn't collide with existing scripts (e.g. `12-` or `17-`; not `15-` which is taken by xcode). It uses the `run_onchange_after_` prefix and `.sh.tmpl` suffix consistent with siblings. It does NOT renumber any existing scripts.
expectations: Picks a number in the gap between 10 and 20 that doesn't collide with the existing 15-xcode script
Uses the `run_onchange_after_` prefix matching the surrounding scripts in this number range
Uses `.sh.tmpl` suffix if templating is needed (or `.sh` if not — consistent with siblings)
Does NOT renumber any existing scripts (e.g. does not propose changing `15-xcode` to `20-xcode`)
Notes that script content hash drives re-run, not the filename — the new script will run when content changes
Suggests `chezmoi apply --dry-run --verbose --include=scripts` to verify ordering
References the repo's numeric-ordering convention from SKILL.md Repo-Specific Gotchas or workflow.md

evals.name: chezmoi-diff-direction-walkthrough
evals.prompt:
`chezmoi diff ~/.zshrc` shows me this:

```
--- a/home/dot_zshrc
+++ b/home/dot_zshrc
@@ -1,3 +1,3 @@
 export EDITOR=nvim
-alias gst='git status'
+alias gs='git status'
```

Walk me through what this means and what `chezmoi apply` will do. Will I lose the `gst` alias?
evals.expected_output: The agent explains the direction convention: `-` is content currently in the destination, `+` is the computed target state. `chezmoi apply` will REMOVE the `-` line and ADD the `+` line, so the user's `gst` alias will be replaced by `gs`. The agent identifies this as drift and offers the resolution options (re-add to capture gst into source, accept apply to pick up gs, or merge). It does NOT confidently invert the direction.
expectations: Explains that `-` lines are content currently in the destination (`~/.zshrc`)
Explains that `+` lines are content in the computed target state (source after .tmpl expansion)
States explicitly: `chezmoi apply` will REMOVE the `-` line (`alias gst=...`) and ADD the `+` line (`alias gs=...`) — the user's `gst` alias WILL be lost on apply
Identifies the situation as drift (user edited dest; source has the canonical alias)
Offers at least one resolution: re-add to capture `gst` into source, OR accept apply to pick up `gs`, OR `chezmoi merge` for a three-way
Suggests `diff <(chezmoi cat ~/.zshrc) ~/.zshrc` or similar to sidestep the +/- confusion
Does NOT invert the direction (does NOT say apply preserves the user's gst alias)
